center.label: Cloud-native geospatial
center.summary: A question-first roadmap built from the local SDS course, moving from setup and spatial fundamentals into modelling and raster-vector analysis. Lampata cloud-native additions appear only as secondary extension tools.

questions.label: How do I work in the course environment?
questions.summary: The course starts with the practical environment: terminal basics, a reproducible Python stack, and notebook-based work in Jupyter Lab.

subtopics.label: Terminal and navigation
subtopics.summary: Students learn to move through folders, inspect files, and run the commands needed to launch the course workflow.

subtopics.label: Jupyter Lab and notebooks
subtopics.summary: The core teaching format is notebook-first: code, text, outputs, and markdown in one place.

subtopics.label: Reproducible Python environment
subtopics.summary: The course environment is installed with Pixi and documented with a conda fallback, so the stack can be recreated consistently.

questions.label: What is a projection?
questions.summary: A projection turns locations on the earth into coordinates on a flat surface, which is why distance, area, and overlay only make sense in the right CRS.

subtopics.label: CRS
subtopics.summary: Coordinate reference systems define how geometry is located, measured, and transformed.

subtopics.label: Projected vs geographic coordinates
subtopics.summary: Longitude and latitude are not interchangeable with planar x and y, so measurements and joins need the correct CRS.

subtopics.label: Measurement and reprojection
subtopics.summary: Area, distance, and buffering only become meaningful once data is reprojected into an appropriate analysis CRS.

subtopics.label: DGGS and hexagonal indexing
subtopics.summary: Discrete global grid systems offer another way to represent the earth by indexing it into consistent cells, with H3 being a common practical example.

questions.label: Raster or vector?
questions.summary: Some questions are about discrete features and boundaries, while others are about grids, surfaces, and cell values.

subtopics.label: Feature geometry
subtopics.summary: Points, lines, and polygons are explicit geometries with attributes and boundaries.

subtopics.label: Cells, resolution, and extent
subtopics.summary: Raster data stores values on a grid, so cell size and extent control what can be seen and compared.

subtopics.label: Choosing the right representation
subtopics.summary: The data model should follow the question: vector for bounded objects, raster for continuous surfaces and dense measurements.

questions.label: How do I visualise data?
questions.summary: Visualisation is part of analysis: the right view helps teams inspect spatial patterns, compare layers, and communicate what matters without hiding scale or uncertainty.

subtopics.label: Static maps and basemap context
subtopics.summary: Good static maps combine the analytical layer with just enough contextual basemap and styling to make location and scale readable.

subtopics.label: Raster views and band composites
subtopics.summary: Raster visualisation often means choosing bands, scaling values, and preparing views that make patterns visible without misrepresenting the data.

subtopics.label: Large-data rendering
subtopics.summary: When datasets are too dense to draw point-by-point, aggregation and screen-scale rendering become essential for usable visual outputs.

questions.label: How do cloud-native geospatial workflows run in the cloud?
questions.summary: This extension module focuses on the general delivery pattern: start with manageable cloud building blocks, keep assets in object storage, and run packaged compute close to the data.

subtopics.label: Getting started with cloud
subtopics.summary: A practical cloud starting point is usually simple: pick object storage, one managed compute option, and one repeatable workflow before reaching for more complex orchestration.

subtopics.label: Object storage and remote assets
subtopics.summary: Cloud-native workflows start with remote assets that can be read in place, so teams avoid copying full datasets down for every task.

subtopics.label: Spatial databases and operational layers
subtopics.summary: PostGIS is often the right home for curated vector layers, queryable spatial tables, and application-facing data products that need to stay live rather than sit only in files.

subtopics.label: Containerized compute and scheduled runs
subtopics.summary: Once data is remote, larger analyses are usually packaged into containers or scheduled jobs so the same workflow can be rerun reliably.

subtopics.label: Kubernetes basics
subtopics.summary: Kubernetes gives teams the core deployment model for running containerized geospatial workloads as scalable services, jobs, and shared platforms.

subtopics.label: Deploying Pangeo
subtopics.summary: Pangeo is a practical pattern for shared notebook environments, scalable chunked-array analysis, and collaborative cloud-native EO workflows.

questions.label: Which cloud-native geospatial formats fit which jobs and data cubes?
questions.summary: There is no single best format: the right choice depends on whether teams need raster scenes, data cubes, analytical vector tables, or tiles built for fast web maps.

subtopics.label: Raster scenes and data cubes
subtopics.summary: COGs suit raster scenes and range reads over a network, while Zarr is built for chunked multidimensional arrays and parallel access.

subtopics.label: Vector analysis and feature delivery
subtopics.summary: GeoParquet is strong for analytical vector tables, while FlatGeobuf is useful when fast streaming or spatially indexed feature access matters.

subtopics.label: Visualization-ready tiles
subtopics.summary: PMTiles packages many map tiles into one cloud-friendly archive, which makes it useful for delivery and visualization rather than full analytical geometry work.

questions.label: How do spatial relationships work?
questions.summary: Spatial analysis encodes adjacency, overlap, and neighborhood structure rather than treating each record as an isolated row.

subtopics.label: Contiguity and neighbors
subtopics.summary: Queen and rook relationships turn shared boundaries into explicit neighborhood definitions.

subtopics.label: Joins, buffers, and overlays
subtopics.summary: Spatial joins, buffers, and overlays connect features by where they intersect, touch, or contain one another.

subtopics.label: Graph structure
subtopics.summary: A spatial graph is the formal structure that stores who is linked to whom and how strongly.

questions.label: Why does autocorrelation matter?
questions.summary: Nearby places often behave similarly, so spatial randomness is the exception rather than the default.

subtopics.label: Spatial lag
subtopics.summary: Spatial lag summarizes the values around each observation using a neighborhood graph.

subtopics.label: Global autocorrelation
subtopics.summary: Global statistics test whether the whole map looks more clustered or dispersed than chance would suggest.

subtopics.label: Local clusters and outliers
subtopics.summary: Local indicators show where hot spots, cold spots, and spatial outliers depart from the broader pattern.

questions.label: How do points form patterns?
questions.summary: Point data is about events, density, and spatial arrangement, not just isolated coordinates on a map.

subtopics.label: Density and hotspots
subtopics.summary: Hexbinning and density estimation reveal where events accumulate instead of showing every point one by one.

subtopics.label: Centrography
subtopics.summary: Mean centers, medians, and ellipses summarize the tendency and spread of an entire point pattern.

subtopics.label: Event clustering
subtopics.summary: Point patterns can also be grouped into clusters that help describe recurring spatial structure.

questions.label: How do we group similar places?
questions.summary: Clustering reduces several variables into a smaller set of interpretable area types or regions.

subtopics.label: Multivariate attributes
subtopics.summary: Clustering starts with several variables that describe each area, not a single metric.

subtopics.label: K-means clustering
subtopics.summary: K-means groups observations into a chosen number of clusters based on attribute similarity.

subtopics.label: Regionalisation
subtopics.summary: Regionalisation adds spatial constraints so neighboring areas stay together when forming regions.

questions.label: How do we estimate across space?
questions.summary: Interpolation redistributes or predicts values across space, but each method carries assumptions about what the data represents.

subtopics.label: Areal interpolation
subtopics.summary: Area-weighted and pycnophylactic methods move values from one set of polygons to another based on overlap and smoothing assumptions.

subtopics.label: Point interpolation
subtopics.summary: Interpolation from sparse points estimates values at new locations or across a surface.

subtopics.label: Estimation assumptions
subtopics.summary: Support, smoothing, neighborhood choice, and target geometry all shape whether an interpolated result is credible.

questions.label: How do we explain variation with regression?
questions.summary: The regression chapter moves from baseline OLS into spatial residual structure, heterogeneity, and geographically weighted regression.

subtopics.label: OLS models
subtopics.summary: The course starts with non-spatial linear regression as the baseline for explaining geographic variation.

subtopics.label: Residual structure
subtopics.summary: Residual maps and Moran-style diagnostics expose the spatial structure that a simple regression still misses.

subtopics.label: Spatial heterogeneity and GWR
subtopics.summary: Spatial fixed effects and geographically weighted regression show how relationships vary across space.

questions.label: How do we evaluate spatial models?
questions.summary: The tree-regression chapter is really about evaluation: train-test splits, overfitting, cross-validated prediction, residual maps, and spatial leakage.

subtopics.label: Train-test split and overfitting
subtopics.summary: The course compares linear and random-forest models, checks train versus test scores, and tunes hyperparameters to reduce overfitting.

subtopics.label: Cross-validated prediction and residuals
subtopics.summary: Cross-validated prediction makes full-map residual analysis possible without training on the same observations being evaluated.

subtopics.label: Spatial leakage and model comparison
subtopics.summary: Spatially stratified metrics, dependency of error, spatial cross-validation, and model comparison are treated as core evaluation practice.

questions.label: How do we classify places in space?
questions.summary: The classification chapter adds categorical prediction, spatial cross-validation, and spatial feature engineering to the workflow.

subtopics.label: Random forest classifier
subtopics.summary: Classification starts with a baseline random-forest model and moves through class predictions, probabilities, and accuracy metrics.

subtopics.label: Spatial cross-validation
subtopics.summary: The course shows why ordinary validation is not enough when neighbouring observations leak information across folds.

subtopics.label: Feature engineering
subtopics.summary: Map synthesis, proximity variables, spatially lagged features, and x-y coordinates strengthen classification in space.

questions.label: How do rasters represent the world and connect back to vectors?
questions.summary: The raster chapter treats grids as labelled arrays, then shows how rasters can be clipped, resampled, extracted, and summarized back into vector analysis.

subtopics.label: Arrays and raster metadata
subtopics.summary: Rasters are handled as labelled arrays with bounds, CRS, nodata, bands, and file structure that matter analytically.

subtopics.label: Clipping, resampling, and remote rasters
subtopics.summary: Large rasters become practical when they are clipped to an area of interest and resampled to a sensible display or processing scale.

subtopics.label: Extraction and zonal statistics
subtopics.summary: The chapter finishes by extracting values at points, computing zonal statistics over polygons, and turning raster cubes back into vector-friendly tables.

technologies.label: Shell
technologies.summary: The course begins in the terminal, using shell commands to navigate folders and launch the notebook workflow.

technologies.label: Pixi
technologies.summary: Sets up the reproducible Python environment used throughout the course.

technologies.label: JupyterLab
technologies.summary: The primary notebook environment used for the hands-on parts of the course.

technologies.label: pyproj
technologies.summary: Inspect and transform coordinate reference systems in Python.

technologies.label: GeoPandas
technologies.summary: The main Python workhorse for vector geometry, spatial tables, and CRS-aware analysis.

technologies.label: pyogrio
technologies.summary: Fast vector IO used in practical GeoPandas workflows.

technologies.label: Shapely
technologies.summary: Core geometry operations such as buffers, intersections, validity checks, and centroids.

technologies.label: libpysal
technologies.summary: Build spatial graphs, neighborhood structures, and weights matrices.

technologies.label: esda
technologies.summary: Compute global and local spatial autocorrelation statistics.

technologies.label: pandas
technologies.summary: Prepares attribute tables and feature matrices before clustering and modelling.

technologies.label: pointpats
technologies.summary: Analyze point density, centrography, and point pattern structure.

technologies.label: h3-py
technologies.summary: Supports hexagonal grids and target geometries used in point-pattern and interpolation exercises.

technologies.label: DGGS
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: discrete global grid systems partition the earth into indexed cells for consistent global analysis and aggregation.

technologies.label: contextily
technologies.summary: Add basemap context to static spatial plots.

technologies.label: tobler
technologies.summary: Supports areal interpolation and related spatial estimation workflows.

technologies.label: pyinterpolate
technologies.summary: Dedicated interpolation tooling for estimating values across space.

technologies.label: statsmodels
technologies.summary: Provides the formula-based OLS workflow used in the regression chapter.

technologies.label: mgwr
technologies.summary: Supports geographically weighted regression and related local modelling.

technologies.label: scikit-learn
technologies.summary: Provides clustering, supervised learning, metrics, and validation across the later course chapters.

technologies.label: xarray
technologies.summary: Treat raster and gridded data as labelled arrays with named dimensions.

technologies.label: rioxarray
technologies.summary: Adds geospatial raster IO and a rio accessor to xarray objects.

technologies.label: rasterio
technologies.summary: Handles raster metadata, file access, masking, and grid-level properties.

technologies.label: datashader
technologies.summary: Resamples large rasters for screen-scale visualization without plotting every original cell.

technologies.label: geocube
technologies.summary: Bridges raster and vector workflows for extraction and zonal statistics.

technologies.label: xvec
technologies.summary: Links raster-style arrays back to points, polygons, and geospatial tables.

technologies.label: osmnx
technologies.summary: Supplies boundary and network context used in the raster-vector part of the course.

technologies.label: STAC
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: query large remote imagery collections without treating discovery as a manual step.

technologies.label: Pangeo
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: collaborative cloud-native geoscience pattern built around shared environments, open data access, and scalable array computing.

technologies.label: Kubernetes
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: orchestrate containerized services, jobs, and shared platforms for scalable cloud-native geospatial delivery.

technologies.label: Object storage
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: bucket or blob storage used to host large geospatial assets for remote access and durable outputs.

technologies.label: Containerized compute
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: package geospatial code and dependencies into repeatable jobs or services that can run on managed container platforms.

technologies.label: Scheduled jobs
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: batch or scheduled execution for refreshes, repeatable processing, and larger analytical runs.

technologies.label: Cloud basics
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: the entry-level cloud pattern of combining storage, managed compute, and a small repeatable workflow before scaling up architecture.

technologies.label: COG
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: use cloud-optimized GeoTIFF layouts for efficient remote access.

technologies.label: Zarr
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: chunked, compressed n-dimensional arrays that support partial reads and parallel processing from object storage.

technologies.label: AWS S3
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: object storage pattern for hosted raster assets and cloud-native delivery.

technologies.label: Dask
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: chunked and lazy computation for larger raster workflows than the core SDS material emphasizes.

technologies.label: GeoParquet
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: portable geospatial columnar storage for vector outputs and derived tables.

technologies.label: PostGIS
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: spatial database layer for storing, querying, and serving operational vector data with SQL.

technologies.label: FlatGeobuf
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: binary vector format with optional spatial indexing that supports fast remote reads and feature streaming.

technologies.label: PMTiles
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: single-file tiled archive optimized for map delivery and visualization through HTTP range requests.

technologies.label: DuckDB
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: lightweight analytical querying for raster-derived tables and vector outputs.

technologies.label: Azure Blob Storage
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: object storage for cloud-hosted rasters, derived tables, and other geospatial assets.

technologies.label: Azure Container Apps
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: managed container runtime for packaging geospatial APIs, scheduled jobs, and internal services.

technologies.label: Azure Functions
technologies.summary: Lampata extension: event-driven or scheduled execution for small geospatial processing steps and automation hooks.
